summary: This source outlines Thomas Edison's philosophical, religious, and economic views, alongside brief biographical details. It highlights his belief in 'Nature' as a supreme intelligence rather than a personal religious deity, his view of humans as aggregates of cells, and his strong opposition to debt-based monetary systems and interest.

principles.statement: What people call God is actually Nature, a supreme intelligence that rules matter, not a merciful or loving personal deity.
principles.rationale: Observation of the natural world shows it is not inherently kind or merciful (e.g., the food chain), suggesting that a supreme intelligence governs matter without human-like emotions.
principles.source_quote: what you call God I call Nature, the Supreme intelligence that rules matter.

principles.statement: The government should issue credit-based money rather than debt-based money.
principles.rationale: It is absurd for taxpayers to be compelled to pay double the principal in interest when the government has the power to produce money as a credit instead.
principles.source_quote: if the government can produce debt-based money, it could equally as well produce money that was a credit to the taxpayer.

mental_models.name: The Human as an Aggregate
mental_models.description: Viewing a human being not as a singular, indivisible entity or soul, but as a collective aggregate of smaller living components (cells).
mental_models.example: Edison compared a human being to New York City, asking 'Will New York City go to heaven?' to illustrate the absurdity of an aggregate entity having an afterlife.

signature_quotes.text: I am not an individual—I am an aggregate of cells, as, for instance, New York City is an aggregate of individuals. Will New York City go to heaven?
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his views on individuality, the soul, and the afterlife.

signature_quotes.text: Nature is what we know. We do not know the gods of religions. And nature is not kind, or merciful, or loving.
signature_quotes.context: From an October 2, 1910, interview in The New York Times Magazine regarding his religious beliefs.

signature_quotes.text: Gold is a relic of Julius Caesar, and interest is an invention of Satan.
signature_quotes.context: Quoted in the New York Times discussing his desire for monetary reform.

signature_quotes.text: There is no such denial, what you call God I call Nature, the Supreme intelligence that rules matter.
signature_quotes.context: Clarifying his views in a private letter after being publicly labeled an atheist.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on debt-based monetary systems and charging interest.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates an absurd system where taxpayers are compelled to pay back double the principal or more, when the government could simply issue credit-based, interest-free currency.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in a personal, merciful deity that grants eternal life to the individual soul.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Nature demonstrates a lack of mercy (e.g., humans catching and eating fish), and humans are merely aggregates of cells that will likely disperse back into nature upon death rather than living on as a singular entity.
